div.head: The Secretary of State to the Minister Resident in Saudi Arabia (Moose)In telegram 224, July 29, 1 p.m., the Minister Resident informed the Department that “arrangements are being made for Jordan and me to go to Riyadh August 1st to deliver to the King message contained in the Department’s telegram No. 149 …” (890F.24/7–2644)
div.p:
149. The British Foreign Office desires certain revisions in the text of the joint instructions contained in Department’s 128, July 11, 2
p.m. The Foreign Office’s revised version has been revised further after discussions between officers of the Department and officials of the British Embassy, which is telegraphing the text of the revision of the Foreign Office’s version to London for approval. If the Foreign Office approves of the text thereof as agreed upon in Washington the following instructions will be telegraphed mutatis mutandis by the Foreign Office to your British colleague and in that event the following text constitutes the joint instructions to you and to him regarding your forthcoming visit to King Ibn Saud in connection with the supply program, in lieu of the instructions contained in Department’s no. 128, July 11, 2 p.m.:
“In view of Ibn Saud’s recent urgent appeal to British and American Governments for aid it be considered essential that the King be informed as soon as possible that both Governments have reached agreement on a joint aid programme worked out recently in Cairo. You should therefore arrange to visit the King as soon as possible and in any event not later than Tuesday, 1st of August, to apprize him thereof. You should also invite your British colleague to accompany you on that journey in order that this information may be conveyed to the King jointly. At the time of your visit you should make to the King a communication in the following sense but so worded as to take account of His Majesty’s susceptibilities:—
A. As Ibn Saud is aware, a schedule of all supplies essential for Saudi Arabia for 1944 has been prepared by MESC. Within that framework His Majesty’s Government and the U.S. Government have decided for their part to furnish Ibn Saud with certain supplies. They have worked out a joint supply programme for 1944 the aim of which will be to maintain a 3 months’ supply of cereals, tea and sugar in Saudi Arabia at all times. The list of supplies which comprise this programme is contained in the appendix to these instructions.
B. The joint nature of this supply programme should be made clear by emphasizing that the total expense of this programme is to be shared in equal measure by the American and British Governments, irrespective of the countries of origin of the supplies, which will be procured in accordance with sound economic practice and with utmost regard to economy of shipping space.
C. As result of this joint American-British programme 150 trucks have recently been or shortly will be delivered to Saudi Arabia and it is assumed that these trucks will assist materially in the immediate problem of food distribution. Furthermore it is the intention of H.M. Government and the American Government to do all they can to continue to supply Saudi Arabia with sufficient motor vehicles, spare parts and tires to meet essential transportation needs.In telegram 227, July 31, 11 a.m., the Minister Resident reported that paragraph C was being omitted from the joint United States-British communication to King Ibn Saud because of discrepancies in the instructions received from the two Governments (890F.24/7–3144).
D. It should be emphasized to the King that the supply programme for 1944 contemplates that he will sell an increased proportion of the
goods supplied to him. It should be explained also that as oil company operations expand, and as the pilgrimage traffic increases with improvement in transportation facilities, His Majesty’s Government and the U.S. Government will expect the Saudi Arabian Government and people to become increasingly self-supporting.
Firstly, employment of Saudi Arab workers by the oil company together with sale of services and profits from sale of goods to increased numbers of pilgrim visitors, should increase the ability of the Saudi Arabian people either to purchase supplies from the government, or to finance imports of goods through normal commercial channels. Thus a lesser proportion of the essential needs of the Saudi Arab people will have to be met by free distribution of goods or money subsidies by the government.
Secondly, the revenues of the government itself should increase, both from sale of more goods to the people, and from increased pilgrimage tariffs and oil royalties. This will enable the government to pay for a greater proportion of any goods supplied under a 1945 or any subsequent joint British-American supply program, and should also provide the King with sufficient money revenues to cover his essential needs.
E. It should be pointed out that it is essential that British and American Governments should be provided with fullest possible statistics regarding supply and consumption in Saudi Arabia to enable them to estimate what help is required. The MESC has to judge within the limits of shipping and supplies available for the Middle East between conflicting demands of a number of countries. It can only decide fairly on basis of accurate statistics.
F. H.M. Government and American Government have given full and sympathetic consideration to Ibn Saud’s present difficulties and they believe that with the assistance mentioned above His Majesty will be able to overcome his supply and budgetary difficulties.
G. Should the King enquire whether supplies provided for in 1944 programme are actually being delivered you should reply that shipments of cereals, sugar and tea to date have been made according to schedule. If you have concrete information about delivery of other commodities you may tell Ibn Saud that also.
Saudi Arabia subsidised joint Anglo-American programme 1944

In addition, the following will be provided as part of the joint programme:— (a)
His Majesty’s Government will provide £10,000 a month to cover the expenses of Ibn Saud’s Legations and Consulates as from July 1st, 1944 (these expenses have been otherwise covered down to June 30th). (b)
The United States Government will provide 10,000,000 silver riyals on credit Lend-Lease.”

In view of the foregoing you should disregard the instructions contained in Department’s no. 128 from the beginning of that telegram up to but not including lettered paragraph (h), which concerns Aramco funds. The instructions contained in no. 128 from lettered paragraph (h) through ensuing numbered paragraphs 2 and 3 thereof ending “accompanying Jordan” although not a part of the joint instructions remain effective as individual instructions to you, transmitted to you with the full knowledge and concurrence of British officials in Washington.

It has been agreed in Washington by the Department and the British Embassy that the joint visit shall be made not later than August 1st in view of the King’s anxiety regarding the supply situation. It has been agreed furthermore that, in the event either you or your British colleague is personally unable to make the visit because of illness or other personal reasons, the senior subordinate member of your or his staff should proceed in your or his stead.
It was also agreed that the joint official party should be kept as small as possible, preferably including no officials other than you, your British colleague, Gunter and Bailey.
Please forward text of this message to Cairo for the information of AEMME.
